card.intuition: A heap is a complete binary tree stored as an array. Min-heap invariant: every parent ≤ its children (root = minimum). Max-heap: every parent ≥ children (root = maximum). Parent of i is at (i-1)//2; children of i are at 2i+1 and 2i+2.
card.analogy: A corporate hierarchy where every manager earns less (min-heap) or more (max-heap) than their direct reports. The CEO (root) is always the extreme. New hires bubble up if they break the pay rule.
card.time_complexity: O(log n) push and pop; O(1) peek at root
when_to_use: Priority queues — always need the minimum or maximum element
Heapsort — in-place O(n log n) sort
k-th largest/smallest element; median maintenance with two heaps
gotchas: Python's heapq is a MIN-heap — negate values for max-heap behaviour: push(-val)
After arbitrary element change (decrease-key / increase-key), sift up or sift down to restore invariant

exercise.prompt: Implement sift-up (bubble up) for a min-heap after inserting a new element at the end.
hints: Parent of node at index i is at index (i-1)//2 in 0-indexed array representation
exercise.test_code:
import heapq, random
heap = MinHeap()
vals = [5,3,8,1,9,2,7,4,6]
for v in vals:
    heap.push(v)
assert heap.peek() == 1
result = [heap.pop() for _ in range(len(vals))]
assert result == sorted(vals)

# Compare with Python heapq
pq = vals[:]
heapq.heapify(pq)
py_result = [heapq.heappop(pq) for _ in range(len(vals))]
assert result == py_result
print('PASS')
